# Transcrição: 02-Behind the Scenes with a Mastermind Facilitator April Roberts

- **Fonte:** 02-Behind the Scenes with a Mastermind Facilitator April Roberts
- **Duração:** 23:14
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hello and welcome. I'm Lane Amon. I'm Director of Content and Mastermind.com and I'm welcoming April Roberts to the Mastermind Studio. April, welcome. April has been helping people for 16 years through both her financial practice and the Vixen Gathering. What a great name. She started her multi-tiered mastermind and coaching business to serve female entrepreneurs because she was grateful for the decade of masterminds that she had attended as an entrepreneur and she knew that when you gather a group of phenomenal women together, the conversation is just different. April is a keynote speaker, a podcast host and high performance coach who helps her clients scale their businesses intentionally and live the lifestyle they choose. That is amazing. April, thank you so much for joining us here. I'm so excited to be here, Lane. Thank you. Oh my gosh, I can't wait to dive into the world of masterminds. How did you get started with masterminds in particular? So, I'll give you the very short version of the lifetime story. But no, I was an entrepreneur since 2008. I failed myself as an entrepreneur because I'd just gone through a divorce. So, it was kind of this moment where I could start my life again and you know, most of us are very high performance individuals and we never take that time to pause and think about what we really want to do, right? And so, I started my business in 2008, trained with my dad for two years, moved to Houston, opened my own financial offices and then around 2012, I had a strategic partner come and say, hey, you don't know how to run a business. You're a great financial advisor but you don't know how to run a business. Let us help you. So, that year I got to go to Tony Robbins. I went to Bo Eason. I went to Darren Hardy and like they would subsidize and help me go to these masterminds to learn how to be a better business owner and market. And so, that started 2012. So, I've always gone to masterminds since then and what I loved is it helped me keep my sanity as an entrepreneur because if you're a content creator, a coach, you know, a mastermind facilitator, you in essence are a business owner and people don't get what our life is like on a day-to-day basis and sometimes your significant other doesn't even get it, right? So, I always like to say my masterminds help me keep my sanity. Those were like my people that got it, right? So valuable to have those people who accompany you. But then that's a shift to go from enjoying the mastermind and knowing how valuable it is to actually saying, okay, I'm going to start my own mastermind. So, talk about that. Well, I kept getting called to do something other than my financial planning practice, which again, you know, most people watching this are thinking about making a career shift and there's so many layers to that. We won't get into it now, but you have so many people that depend on you. You're responsible for people. It's a big decision and not just yours. But I knew I wanted to make impact in a different way, a more direct way. And so, at the time,

I thought, okay, I'll go speak on stages. Well, here comes 2020 and there are no stages. I was like, great, what do I do now? So, you know, I entered the Vixen gathering thinking I'd be a coach. And what happened honestly is someone recruited me to do masterminds for them. And I started doing the training. As you know, I think them very much. Like I let them keep the fee because I was backing out. And I said, I can just form my own mastermind. And honestly, that's how it started. I just said, you know, I really want to be teaching what I believe in and what I feel women need based on these years of conversing with all of them and us sharing our common problems. Probably, I don't know if it was still 2020 or we are dealing with the virtual stuff. Oh, no, we were, we could be live then. So I think I launched March of last year, my first full year. So first year, I kept it small on purpose because we wanted to make sure, you know, Dean's a big fan of over delivering. I love when he says that because I've always believed in that. So I wanted

to make sure we took care of an over delivered. And with everything you do, again, starting, this is actually my third business was very humbling. And I'm going to have to tweak it and things aren't going to work and we're going to fix it. So I think if you have an adventure's mentality about it, it's really great. It's almost like masterminds become an organism of their own where everybody's dealing with the same thing at once. And it kind of shifts. And you're like, okay, I guess we're going there. So well, it's so true. And also, you know, it's nice with a mastermind because you can make those tweaks. And it leverages your time better. That's one advice I'd give is really understand your boundaries and, you know, for people that have done masterminds for a long time, I think they understand, here's what we do, here's what we don't do. Sometimes when you're new, you're going to get pulled in a lot of directions and be prepared for that and have a good framework of what you do and what you don't do. That is a really good point because you, you know, we, most of us get into this industry because we want to serve people. We want to be responsive. But just because somebody suggests something doesn't mean we should do that or that that's what they really need. So it's this kind of this intuition about, okay, is that in my zone of genius? Is that something that's going to serve everybody? Or is that just this person's having a really bad day so they need XYZ or whatever it might be, but really being discerning about the decisions you make instead of just, okay, they want this, so I'm going to add this and they want that. So I'm going to add that. But really, hey, this mastermind is this and we'll revise, you know, maybe we take a look mid-year and see if there's some big changes that need to be made. But really, this is a program people signed up for and they signed up for it for reasons. So to change everything midstream doesn't make a lot of sense. It's really important to know your audience and cater to that. So I know one thing that shocked me is because I've been doing masterminds a long time. I'm kind of used to writing the high ticket. Not everybody is used to writing, you know, paying for a high ticket. And then, I mean, I remember the hotels would be ridiculously expensive sometimes on top of it. And when I was beginner entrepreneur, that was like a little stressful, like, okay, I want to be there, but that's so expensive. So what was interesting is my mastermind was priced well. And then we had nice hotels, but I tried to keep them like below 400 a night. I thought it was very reasonable. It actually caused an issue a lot of time. So I think, you know, you want the nice venue, but maybe have alternative venues that you can suggest for people to stay at. Because, you know, it's just where we are in the economy right now. Sometimes people, travel's gotten expensive and people stress. And you really want them to be present at your event. What else surprised you about your first mastermind when you first set it up? So I actually framed it so that we could go deep fast. Luckily, when I started the Vixen

## [09:05 - 12:09]

gathering, I did a series of happy hours where I limited it to 16 women so we could set round table. And our masterminds, we set round table, like, or we do the big square, right? So there's no head of the table, no one's up on the stage. This is just how I have it designed. So I facilitated these happy hours. And so I learned how to help the women go deep quickly, right? And so a lot of times you waste time getting deep, which is the power of a mastermind. Because I see it with one-on-one coaching versus small group or mastermind. I actually think clients learn better in the group setting and they accelerate faster because they're learning from you, your speakers, and each other. Whereas when it's one-on-one, a lot of people want to do one-on-one, but they're not hearing anyone but themselves, right? So the evolution actually takes a little bit more time, in my opinion, when you're one-on-one. I agree. I can see that completely because when it's just you and the other person and they make excuses, and then in the group setting, either somebody else will call them out on it and you don't have to, or they hear themselves, or they hear somebody else making excuses and they're like, whoa, my gosh, I do that same thing. So there's a level of accountability and just that synergistic effect in a mastermind group that you don't get with the one-on-one, which I love. I agree. And also, you're learning from other people and you're also, like you said, holding yourself accountable. And a lot of times, especially when you're first an entrepreneur, it's like this mirror in front of your face all the time. And so it can be a lot of deep internal work going on. So while you might want to go to your room right away afterward and just hide from the world, it's really nice to have that support group that you've gone deep with that it's okay to be vulnerable with. I think the most if you run a true mastermind, and it's pretty much you on, I do have a co facilitator. So we'll go back and forth that I carry most of the load. The key is to be a truly good facilitator. And not everyone is because we've all paid a lot of money to go to an event and that one person talks all the time, right? And so you really have to monitor again.

## [12:09 - 15:11]

That's why we sit the formation I have, because it's like, okay, let's go around and share our ahas. Let's share what we got from this. And it's really makes it easier to make sure everyone has a voice in the room and that everyone's talking. So I actually think facilitation is an art that most people don't plan for managing because, you know, you have to sometimes be like, hey, okay, thank you for your advice, but you're just confusing her. She's asking me this question, because this is the problem you run when the group gets very close is really managing the conversation and the flow of the conversation and making sure that everyone is getting what they need. So assertiveness, a little spirit of that bravery to go with, you know, hey, I got to be in the moment and shift as things go along. So for me, I mean, I have an even deeper spiritual purpose to the work I do, but I just saw so many women, you know, I mean, my clients are 35 to 55. They have kids, they have aging parents, they're running a business, they're trying to keep their husband happy, right? Like there's a lot of balls in the air and they're coming last. And so really my purpose is, hey, yeah, I'm really good at helping people break their limited money mindset. So my number one goal is let's be abundant, right? Number two is what lifestyle do you want to get there, right? It's not just about hitting a million dollars, but what does life look like along the path? Do you get to go take a two week vacation with your family? How many days a week do you work? I mean, that's why I just think time management and optimization is so critical because the beauty of being an entrepreneur is you can really craft your life. But a lot of my clients have never been empowered to do that. So how do we create this environment where they're living their best life? And so my mastermind is actually structured like that. filling. I haven't had to say no, but I was happy when someone did not continue after the first year. These things tend to work themselves out, right? Yeah. So the answer to your question, actually what worked for me in the beginning was direct reach out on LinkedIn, which I did not do. I hired someone. And again, I was introduced to them. And now the way we get the most interest is just promoting when we're on our retreats. I mean, you know, just posting the stories. You know, I took a picture of being here like, and people just want to be a part of that. And they're like, literally our last retreat weekend, I think 10 women reached out like, how do I come to the next one? So many people are afraid to be seen starting small, right? Who cares? I had like six women in my first mastermind. I still made it the best. I lost money on the first retreat because I didn't know how to price it. I didn't know how to plan. But like you go forward and then you create this momentum and then more people want to join and the right people want to join. And so don't be afraid. I mean, if there were two women, we would have gone forward with the mastermind. I think if you do something, especially if it's like women's empowerment or something that's not specific about, hey, we're going to help you double your sales or that's not real ROI based. How did you get a sense for what that transformation, what that value was that you provided? So all of my programs do have an ROI. So I am able to charge more because of that. So yes, I did look at industry standards of here's what you can charge for non ROI services, here's ROI, et cetera. So I looked at that and I also knew the mastermind I'd been trained for. I was like, this is way underpriced. Like I just knew that. So I knew I was going to charge more than that. Like it just never felt right to me for the work that went into it. So you want to find out what the industry is doing and then how is what you're doing on par with everyone else or different. And actually Brandy from the mastermind came in and she was like, all of your programs are underpriced here. So what they should be. And so she wants me to double the cost of my mastermind. We haven't done that yet. But I mean, I just kind of looked at what was out there and I priced it for the year and I gave them a discount if they paid in advance, most people do, or they could do two or three. I have changed that just because of what's going on currently. For our mastermind, it's usually women doing multiple six figures and our goal is to get them to seven. So that's like the

## [18:13 - 21:19]

financial goal in that mastermind. And then I have a tier above that for women doing seven and eight figures. And that's more just like a sisterhood. They just want to know other women doing that. So basically I found that the ticket price for the year was a lot for them because they're balancing a lot of balls. And so many women unfortunately have issues with their worth and what they should pay for things and what they should charge, which is a lot of what we work on. So I switched to a quarterly model where it's 90 days because I have a retreat every quarter. And so any rolling 90 days you sign up, you're going to get to go to a retreat. And I actually bumped the price up because per quarter it costs more than the annual cost. And it's great. And already the women are like, okay, when's our next retreat? So they're already thinking about signing up for the next quarter. So yeah, I think you have to feel your way through that to be honest, but start with industry standards and see, do I offer more? Do I offer less? Like I wanted to offer a luxury experience. So you wanted a price tag on it that would reflect that. And really just, you know, my first tier is priced way like 25% of the value it should be on purpose because that's my loss leader to get women in the door and then ascend to the mastermind and then ascend to the third tier. amazing. That's inconvenient for everyone. Who would this not be a good business model for? So I was coaching a group of advisors the other day.
